## Annex 1 - Term Definitions
<!-- Original visible header: ANNEX 1; C-M(2015)0041-REV2; original page marker 1-15 -->
| # | Term | Definition |
|---:|---|---|
| 98 | Service Quality | The ability of a service to provide the intended value. This is different and more generic than the term ‘Quality of Service’ which is essentially used for technical communications layer metrics. Source: ITIL |
| 99 | Service User | A person who uses the IT service on a day-to-day basis. Users are distinct from customers, as some customers do not use the IT service directly. Source: ITIL |
| 100 | Software As A Service (SaaS) | The capability provided to the consumer is to use the provider’s applications running on a cloud infrastructure. The applications are accessible from various client devices through either a thin client interface, such as a web browser (e.g., web-based email), or a program interface. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure including network, servers, operating systems, storage, or even individual application capabilities, with the possible exception of limited user-specific application configuration settings. Source: “The NIST Definition of Cloud Computing”, Peter Mell and Timothy Grance (September 2011). National Institute of Standards and Technology: US Department of Commerce. Special publication 800-145. |
| 101 |  |  |

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 2/800 -->
<!-- Original visible header: ANNEX 1; C-M(2015)0041-REV2; original page marker 1-16 -->
| # | Term | Definition |
|---:|---|---|
| 102 |  |  |
| 103 | Supplier | A third party responsible for supplying goods or services that are required to deliver IT services. Examples of suppliers include commodity hardware and software vendors, network and telecom providers, and outsourcing organizations. Source: ITIL |
| 104 | Taxonomy | A classification schema that arranges terms (e.g. from a controlled vocabulary) into a hierarchy, frequently using a super type-sub type relationship. Source: C-(2008)0113(INV) |
| 105 | Technology Architecture | Set of artefacts describing the hardware, software and network infrastructure needed to support the deployment of the application systems. |
| 106 | Topic Map | A topic map can represent information using topics (representing any concept, from people, countries, and organizations to software modules, individual files, and events), associations (which represent the relationships between them), and occurrences (which represent relationships between topics and information resources relevant to them). An ISO standard (ISO 13250:2003) for the representation and interchange of knowledge, with an emphasis on the findability of information. Source: C-(2008)0113(INV) |

## Annex 2 - Information and Communications Technology Service Management Policy
### INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATIONS TECHNOLOGY

### SERVICE MANAGEMENT POLICY

### Background

1. The vision of the Alliance C3 Strategy1 is for Information and Communications Technology (ICT) Services to underpin the NATO’s Strategic Concept. As such ICT Services should be considered strategic assets and must be managed to maximize mission efficiency and effectiveness in support of Consultation, Command and Control (C3) whilst taking into account the trade-off between cost, functionality, and performance.

2. The employment of systems as services requires a management continuum for the design, provision and use of services. Service Management2 provides the framework for all services in the NATO Enterprise and in a federation throughout their lifecycle, applying best practices fully aligned with the needs of the NATO.

### Aim

3. This policy establishes mandatory principles in terms of provision, use, and control, of services provided within NATO through an approach based on the Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL).

4. The policy is intended as a tool to enable stakeholders to coordinate, deliver and use an efficient and effective set of services matching the needs of the NATO Enterprise while exercising financial responsibility.

### Scope

5. This policy encompasses the management and support of all ICT services delivered within the NATO Enterprise, regardless of their source or funding. This includes, among others, NATO-owned, NATO-leased, nationally and commercially provided ICT services.

### Principles

6. Capabilities Delivered Through Services. All NATO apportioned ICT capabilities shall be defined and provided as services and managed in accordance with this policy.

7. Governance. Service Management shall take input in the form of direction, monitoring and control from NATO governance bodies, including senior policy committees, supervisory boards, and programme steering committees.

**Footnotes:**

1. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018.

2. Management of ICT Services includes the control aspect of the NATO recognized term ‘Service Management and Control’. For this reason this policy simply uses the term ‘Service Management’.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 4/800 -->
8. Adoption of ITIL. In order to achieve a service-based ICT environment, NATO shall adopt ITIL as a service management framework, complemented by other industry best practices. The individual service lifecycle processes shall be aligned with the specific operational requirements of NATO.

9. Roles. Service Management in NATO shall be based on clearly defined, harmonised and standardised roles, processes and functions. All organizational elements in NATO shall be categorised as Service Providers and/or Service Customers. All individuals within NATO bodies are categorised as Service Users.

10. Coordination of Requirements and Planning. Service requirements shall be coordinated amongst the Service Customers to meet operational and business needs and aligned with financial, managerial and policy frameworks, prior to being addressed to the Service Provider. Coordination shall be undertaken to prioritize of service provision via periodic, collective planning organised around the major Communities of Interest enabling functions. In both NATO Enterprise and federation contexts services should be identified against targets for incorporation in a service pipeline.

11. Service Catalogues and Portfolio. All services shall be planned though a coherent Enterprise Service Portfolio and made available through one or more Service Catalogues.

12. Need for Agreements and Contracts. The provision of services must be efficient, effective and measurable. Prior to commencement of any service delivery, Service Providers and Service Customers shall establish between them an agreed contract for all services to be provided, supported by Service Level Agreements, Memoranda of Agreement/Understanding, and appropriate metrics, such as Service Level Targets and Key Performance Indicators.

13. Costs. All provided services must be costed. This includes all costs associated with the Service Lifecycle as well as the risks accepted by the Service Provider and covered under warranty.

14. Interoperability and Standardisation. To promote service management interoperability between services within NATO and at the interconnection with external entities, Service Management shall be based on NATO-agreed standards applicable to the provision, use and monitoring of all services in the Enterprise.

15. Multiple Service Providers. The provision of a service to a Service Customer may involve multiple Service Providers. Standardisation and reutilisation of existing services shall increase interoperability between services from different sources.

16. Service Monitoring and Improvement. To ensure service quality and interoperability, Service Management shall identify and provide suitable management tools, metrics and measurement methods to report and assess the level of service requested and provided. The distributed, interconnected set of organisational relationships within the NATO Enterprise and through interface points to Nations mean operations dependent upon federated services from several sources are particularly vulnerable to manipulation, degradation or loss of infrastructure. Feedback shall be provided to the Service Provider and appropriate regulatory bodies for assessment and service improvement. Service

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 5/800 -->
management regulatory documents will provide the necessary guidance for monitoring and management of services.

### Roles

17. Service Users are responsible for:

17.1. defining service requirements in respect to their operational and business needs;

17.2. providing feedback to the Service Customer and Service Provider(s) to enable service improvement.

18. Service Customers are responsible for:

18.1. service resources aspects, including:

18.2. setting service requirements which balance operational and business needs against financial, managerial and policy constraints;

18.3. coordinating service requirements with other Service Customers;

18.4. setting service level targets;

18.5. monitoring service delivery in respect to the business needs based on metrics agreed with the Service Provider;

18.6. service funding aspects, including:

18.6.1. covering the cost for delivered services;

18.6.2. negotiating and signing contracts with the Service Provider(s).

18.7. Service Providers are responsible for:

18.7.1. designing and implementing services in agreement with Service Customer requirements and in compliance with all applicable standards, policies and directives;

18.7.2. negotiating contracts with Service Customers;

18.7.3. owning and managing the risk associated with the provision of services within the scope of the agreement or contract;

18.7.4. monitoring and measuring service performance against agreed metrics, quality and performance indicators;

18.7.5. reporting service performance as required by the Service Customer;

18.7.6. reporting service performance information necessary for other service providers to monitor and maintain services to the desired service levels;

18.7.7. making adjustments to services based on Service Customer and Service User feedback and service performance, as required and agreed with Service Customers;

18.7.8. using and maintaining standardized methods for Service Management;

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 6/800 -->
18.7.9. establishing a single source of information related to Service Management that provides scalable access to all involved stakeholders;

18.7.10. adhering to all principles of information assurance as part of service provision.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 7/800 -->
## Annex 3 - C3 Capabilities and ICT Services Lifecycle Management Policy
### C3 CAPABILITIES AND ICT SERVICES LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT POLICY

### Background

1. Consultation, Command and Control (C3) Capabilities and Information and Communication (ICT) Services play a pivotal role in the overall NATO Defence Planning Process1 and command a substantial portion of the annual collective investment budget.

2. The Collective Targets in the C3 Planning Domain focus on the provision of NATO-wide cost-effective, interoperable and secure C3 Capabilities and ICT Services2 that meet users’ requirements through common funded assets and services in the most effective and efficient manner.

3. The development, implementation, use and evolution of integrated NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services calls for a lifecycle management approach that coordinates the roles and responsibilities of the many stakeholders in this complex process.

### Aim

4. This policy aims to establish a framework for the development, implementation, use and evolution of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services, the roles and responsibilities of the various stakeholders in this process, with a specific focus on NATO organisations participating in the NATO Enterprise3.

5. This policy provides the highest level mandatory principles for the management of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services (including CIS Security and Cyber Defence) through a structured lifecycle approach.

### Scope

6. This policy is intended to be an integral part of the C3 policy landscape and to be compatible with other relevant policies.

7. The scope of this policy covers the full lifecycle of all C3 Capabilities and ICT Services, which will be used by entities of NATO regardless of their source and funding.

8. This policy is mandatory for NATO Enterprise organizations which participate in the provision of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

9. The C3 Capabilities and ICT Services lifecycle is the full range of activities involved in their planning, definition, design, implementation, integration, verification, operation, evolution and disposal. These include:

**Footnotes:**

1. PO(2016)0655, The NATO Defence Planning Process, 24 October 2016

2. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018

3. C-M(2014)0061, The NATO Enterprise Approach for the Delivery of C3 Capabilities and the Provision of ICT Services, 14 Nov 2014

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 8/800 -->
9.1. Support the NDPP processes through the identification of C3 collective target requirements, the identification of the C3 impacts of other collective targets, and the identification of national and multi-national targets related to C3 interoperability.

9.2. Assessment of emerging technologies, operational and support concepts, and implementation and service provisioning alternatives through analysis and experimentation to ensure the effective evolution of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

9.3. Programming and budgeting of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services through the NATO Security Investment Programme (NSIP), Consolidated Resource Proposals and Financial Plans.

9.4. Procurement, implementation and evolution through NSIP and other investment projects, including the transition to operations.

9.5. Operation and support of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services by service users, customers, providers and operators.

9.6. Phase-out of obsolete services and disposal of their associated assets.

### Principles

10. This lifecycle should be aligned with the Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL) lifecycle phases and processes to enable the adaptation of best practices for ICT service management to NATO needs.

11. The coherence and traceability of requirements and resources across the lifecycle shall be enabled through use of the following:

11.1. The NATO C3 Taxonomy4, which shall be governed by the C3 Board and which shall serve as the strategic level of the architecture and portfolio of NATO Enterprise C3 Capabilities and ICT Services. In addition:

11.2. All NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services offered in Customer Catalogues shall be traceable to the NATO C3 Taxonomy and to the requirements specified in 9.1.

11.3. For each service in the C3 Taxonomy the NATO Enterprise organisation(s) assigned as Service Provider shall be identified. In accordance with the NCIO Charter5, the NCIA is the principal Service Provider for NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

11.4. A C3 Integrated Master Plan, which shall include:

11.5. A set of Top Level views showing the alignment of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Service development and implementation with Alliance strategic initiatives and objectives.

11.6. A set of views that outline the plans, risks and issues for development and implementation of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

11.7. A process for maintaining the plan through regular reviews and for using it to elevate issues that require action or guidance from the Nations.

**Footnotes:**

4. AC/322-D(2016)0017, C3 Taxonomy Baseline 2.0, 14 March 2016.

5. C-M(2012)0049, Establishment of the new NATO Communications and Information Organisation, 14 June 2012

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 9/800 -->
11.8. Customer Catalogues for C3 Capabilities and ICT Services, which shall be used as the basis for Service Level Agreements, CIS support to NATO operations.

11.9. A Technical Validation and Verification function to ensure that C3 Capabilities and ICT Services intended to be interoperable shall be documented and made available to enable the development and testing of compatible national and multi-national capabilities prior to deployment.

12. The management of activities across the lifecycle is accomplished by the coordinated NATO Senior C3 stakeholders, in full respect of individual’s agreed roles and responsibilities, aimed at a coherent implementation of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

12.1. The C3 Capability Area Manager shall establish a senior leadership group to facilitate this coordination and a set of working groups to ensure the effective communication of issues, risks and guidance up from and down to the level of individual projects and services.

12.2. The C3 Capability Area Manager shall be responsible for elevating C3-related issues requiring governance decisions to the Nations.

13. NATO Enterprise organisations shall use the C3 Taxonomy, C3 Integrated Master Plan and Customer Catalogues to integrate and satisfy short, mid and long term C3 requirements for translation into ICT Services in a coherent way.

14. In developing C3 Capabilities and ICT Services requirements, NATO Enterprise organisations shall maximise reuse of existing solutions, using proven off-the-shelf solutions, and only developing NATO-unique solutions where no affordable alternative exists, according to Adopt, Buy or Create (ABC) principle.

### Roles and Responsibilities

15. NATO Enterprise entities shall develop appropriate directives for the implementation of this policy.

16. The C3 Board reserves the right to request evidence on a regular basis for the compliance to this policy to NATO Enterprise entities through the relevant parent body.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 10/800 -->

## Annex 4 - Waveform Policy
### WAVEFORM POLICY

### Background

1. Wireless Networks support Alliance Operations and Missions for a variety of tasks, from strategic to tactical levels of command and laterally at each level. The level of wireless interoperability, especially at tactical levels, is limited and does not fully support the richness of secure communications services required for modern operations. Furthermore developments in Software Defined Radio (SDR) technology provide opportunities that can ease the processes of waveform development and subsequent implementation.

### Aim

2. This Policy provides the principles aimed at improving wireless interoperability through the exploitation of software defined radio technologies and introduction of modern interoperable waveforms.

3. A long term goal of NATO and NATO Nations shall be to build a library or repository, distributed or centralized, of waveform specifications, waveform functional and interoperability reference software that is accessible by NATO and NATO Nations. Non- NATO Nations shall have access to this repository on a case-by-case basis, to be agreed by the C3 Board. As a short term solution, NATO shall create a registry of waveform artifacts as detailed in Section 4.

### Scope

4. This policy provides guidance than can be used by NATO Nations and other entities that need to interoperate within Alliance Operations, Missions and Exercises using wireless capabilities with regards to waveform standards.

5. This policy is meant for public release.

6. This policy is intended primarily to provide advisory guidance to NATO Nations in order to facilitate the development and implementation of wireless waveform standards, and mandatory guidance for NATO staff and committee activities where applicable.

7. This policy applies to the development and implementation of future wireless waveform specifications foreseen to become NATO standards and does not apply to NATO standards already implemented by NATO Nations.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 12/800 -->
### Principles

### Waveform Standards

8. The number of Alliance-adopted waveform standards as well as modifications to Alliance-adopted waveform standards should be kept to a minimum to help ensure interoperability between various implementations.

9. The introduction of new Alliance-adopted waveform standards shall be based on agreed operational requirements or offer an opportunity for significant enhancement to existing Alliance operational capability.

10. New Alliance-adopted waveform specifications and those selected to be part of the set of existing Alliance-adopted waveform standards shall be free of any charge for use for NATO and NATO Nations.

11. Alliance-adopted waveform specifications shall be divided into mandatory and optional functions; the mandatory functions shall define the minimum interoperability capability.

12. Alliance-adopted waveform specifications shall not prevent the implementation of national encryption.

### Waveform Reference Software

13. Alliance-adopted waveforms shall be supported by a complete waveform specification, and shall be supported in due course by waveform functional reference software and waveform interoperability reference software1.

14. Alliance-adopted waveform functional reference software and waveform interoperability reference software shall be provided free of charge to NATO Nations and shall be freely redistributable within NATO and NATO national governments under agreed predefined conditions2.

15. The waveform functional reference software should be used to provide example instantiations of the Alliance-adopted waveforms and should support further waveform definition work.

16. The waveform interoperability reference software should be used by NATO and NATO Nations to support waveform interoperability testing and further waveform definition work.

17. Additional versions of the waveform functional and interoperability reference software with IPR are acceptable, particularly where they demonstrate an ability to enhance the performance.

18. Alliance-adopted waveform software, except for target waveform software, shall not rely on platform specific signal processing functions or OS specific real time mechanisms. Any interaction and dependency required by the Radio Platform for proper waveform operational use shall be documented.

**Footnotes:**

1. AC/322-N(2015)0001-REV1, Framework for Sharing Common Waveforms, 3 February 2015.

2. It is recognized that industrial use and further development of waveform software may involve costs and/or restrictions not levied on NATO and NATO national users of functional and interoperability reference software, as described in Sections 4.10 and 4.16.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 13/800 -->
19. Alliance-adopted waveform software should be based on open software standards, whenever possible, using the Software Communications Architecture (SCA) to facilitate sharing between nations, and include the use of open Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)3.

20. NATO Nations and industry developing prototype waveform software are encouraged to offer these, with the necessary terms and conditions, for use as Alliance waveform reference software (functional and interoperability).

21. Nations and industry implementing NATO waveforms in base or target waveform software are encouraged to offer these, with the necessary terms and conditions, for use in waveform definition and interoperability testing processes.

22. Nations are encouraged to notify the C3 Board, through the NHQC3S of national development of waveform software (typically prototype, base or target) subject to serve as Alliance software references. Nations and industry developing base and/or target waveform software should offer these to NATO Nations under agreed predefined conditions. NATO shall create a registry of waveform artefacts consisting of links to current NATO Nation waveform libraries.

### Base Waveforms

23. NATO shall endeavour to obtain a base waveform implementation to act as a basis for interoperable implementation, and as a basis for interoperability testing that does not bias interoperability testing towards any specific commercial implementation.

24. IPR may be attached to the endorsed base waveform applications, which shall be evaluated for recognition as licensed under Fair, Reasonable, and Non-discriminatory (FRAND) terms by the community of users in the early steps of the endorsement procedure.

**Footnotes:**

3. It is recognized that NATO Nations and Non-NATO stakeholders (non-NATO Nations, national or multinational programs, and industry, for example) will drive the development of the SCA and APIs and that NATO, at best, will be able to influence the direction of this open standard. This also implies that the custodianship of the SCA will best remain with an international group, supported by Industry.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 14/800 -->
Left blank intentionally
