## Annex 5 - Consultation, Command and Control (C3) Interoperability Policy
### CONSULTATION, COMMAND AND CONTROL (C3) INTEROPERABILITY POLICY

### Reference

A. C-M(2009)0145, NATO Interoperability Policy, 3 Dec 2009

### Background

1. Interoperability is the ability to act together coherently, effectively and efficiently to achieve Allied tactical, operational and strategic objectives (Ref. A).

2. NATO is facing new challenges that require a mature ability to share information and provide the C3 Capabilities and ICT Services necessary to achieve information superiority, and to enhance situational awareness and the timeliness of operational planning and decision-making.

3. The Alliance C3 Strategy1 highlights the need for the Alliance to share information across the NATO Enterprise and between NATO, NATO Nations and Partners.

### Aim

4. The aim of this policy is to define and mandate the measures necessary to achieve and maintain C3 Capabilities and ICT Services interoperability to match Alliance needs.

### Scope

5. This policy identifies the principles and main responsibilities for ensuring the development and effective use of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services for achieving interoperability, supporting the sharing of information across the main dimensions (Ref. A): technical, procedural and human.

6. This policy is mandatory for the NATO Enterprise entities. It is also mandatory for Nations when connecting one of their networks to a NATO network and when joining the federated network implemented for a NATO-led operation as defined in other NATO policies that could apply for federating networks.

### Principles

### Use of an Architectural Approach to provide Coherence

7. NATO C3 Interoperability Requirements (C3 IOR) shall be expressed in terms of the required sharing of information and ICT services and shall be identified and consolidated by

**Footnotes:**

1. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 16/800 -->
the NATO Military Authorities (NMA) and Staffs within NATO capability requirement statements for execution by NATO and Nations.

8. Architecture products shall serve to inform, guide and document interoperability of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services in their lifecycle.

### Identification of Standards and Profiles as the basis for Interoperability Solutions

9. Standards and profiles shall be included within the NATO Interoperability Standards and Profiles (NISP).

10. NATO Enterprise entities shall ensure the service interface profiles associated with the C3 Capabilities and ICT Services they develop and provide are published in the NISP and are available for verification and validation testing to other NATO Enterprise entities and NATO Nations.

11. NATO architectures shall utilise the agreed standards (STANAGs) and profiles from the NISP as appropriate to achieve the required interoperability of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

12. Appropriate interoperability solutions and procedures to match C3 IOR over time shall be identified/developed and documented by the implementer and coordinated with the C3 Board as appropriate.

13. NATO Enterprise entities shall implement and adopt the appropriate interoperability solutions and procedures to meet agreed C3 IOR. This will involve the achievement of semantic as well as syntactic, empirical and physical interoperability.

14. Mechanisms shall be made available to ensure that agreed standards are applied in federated service development environments, to ensure the achievement of interoperability.

### Use of common interfaces and standards

15. Service specifications shall define the detailed design characteristics of Core and COI2 services and shall include Service Interface Profiles (SIPs), to ensure the achievement of interoperability across services provided through NATO funded, multi-National and National programmes.

### Verification and validation of Interoperability Solutions through Testing

16. Interoperability of solutions to C3 IOR shall be verified and validated, in a costeffective manner, by testing regularly during the life cycle, in accordance with the provisions of this policy.

17. Testing of the interfaces of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services shall be conducted, including testing against the agreed standards and profiles that are contained within the NISP. Testing at National level is a national responsibility and NATO is responsible for testing as a Host Nation.

18. C3 Capabilities and ICT Services shall have their interfaces pass NATO level C3 Interoperability tests; this testing shall be between NATO, NATO Nations and Partners Nations C3 Capabilities and ICT Services interfaces, based on the NATO agreed standards 2 Community of Interest

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 17/800 -->
and profiles that are contained within the NISP. The testing shall include assessment, analysis, evaluation, verification, validation and up to, but not including, the certification of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

19. The status of interoperability testing of STANAGs is valuable information that must be recorded. To the extent possible, this information shall be included in the NISP.

20. A harmonised spectrum of test capabilities shall be established and used to verify and validate NATO and national C3 interoperability. Test activities shall include technology demonstration and experimentation, standards development and implementation, system interoperability testing, field, pre-deployment and reference system testing.

21. Service Interface Profiles and service interfaces shall be made available for verification and validation testing to NATO Enterprise entities and Nations.

### Roles and Responsibilities

22. National Authorities are responsible for:

22.1. Considering C3 IORs during National programme planning;

22.2. Contributing to, assessing, ratifying and nationally promulgating NATO C3 Standards. Implementation remains a national decision;

22.3. Using C3 Capabilities and ICT Services that conform with FMN C3 Standards when participating in NATO’s Missions3;

22.4. Testing the interfaces of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services at national level, using interoperability test cases and associated test procedures;

22.5. Providing reports on implementation of NATO agreed C3 Standards, using procedures and reports in AAP-03.

23. C3 Board. In co-ordination with other committees as necessary, is responsible for

23.1. Ensuring compliance with the C3 Interoperability Policy and its supporting directive;

23.2. Assessing the C3 Interoperability maturity across the Alliance;

23.3. Coordinating, in close association with Nations and the NATO Commands and Staffs, a programme for NATO C3 Interoperability Testing;

23.4. Ensuring the timely adoption or development of C3 Standards in response to recognised C3 Interoperability shortfalls;

23.5. Ensuring the use of common procedures and practices for developing NATO C3 Standards;

23.6. Ensuring that the NATO Interoperability Standards and Profiles (NISP), is maintained and updates to ADatP-34 are being approved in a timely manner;

23.7. Raising the visibility of C3 interoperability shortfalls and associated C3 standards to the national defence planning level; 3 NATO agreed Interoperability Standards and Profiles as documented in ADaTP-34.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 18/800 -->
23.8. Assisting the MC in the development of MC advice on C3 related interoperability issues;

23.9. Tasking its sub-structure with the development and maintenance of C3 Standards, assigning priorities as required and if requested;

23.10. Ensuring adherence to agreed standards in NATO Technical Service environments and for encouraging Nations to adopt these standards also in the national technical service environment.

24. C3 Board’s Capability Panels (CaPs), supported by Capability Teams (CaTs) are responsible for:

24.1. Developing and maintaining a prioritized list of C3 Standards to be inserted into the Bi-SC Capability Statements and used for formulating common and individual targets in the NATO Defence Planning Process, Step 2;

24.2. Adopting suitable commercial ICT standards or engineering NATO C3 Standards.

25. The Architecture CaT shall support interoperability by verifying C3 IORs, IERs, IRs, required Information and Data Elements, and service exchanges as well as functional, interface and non-functional requirements are coherently and consistently captured. In addition, the Architecture CaT shall maintain a catalogue of Architecture Building Blocks, which provide context for interoperability standards and profiles in the NISP.

26. The Interoperability Profiles CaT shall maintain the NISP in an online format accessible to the public and shall publish baseline updates in document format annually. The IP CaT shall be responsible to classify each C3 Standard in line with agreed taxonomies.

27. NATO Enterprise. NATO Enterprise entities, in particular NATO Military Authorities, are responsible for:

27.1. Implementing the Interoperability Policy;

27.2. Identifying and consolidating the C3 IOR;

27.3. Including appropriate elements of the NATO C3 IOR within the NATO Defence Planning Process and in the development of Capability Packages, to maintain overall integrity of the required interoperable C3 Capabilities and ICT Services;

27.4. Establishing and conducting, as appropriate, in co-ordination with the C3 Board, a programme for NATO C3 Interoperability Testing (including Experimentation, Verification and Validation), Analysis and Feedback, to support the development, fielding, predeployment and operation of C3 interoperable Capabilities and ICT Services.

28. Tasking Authorities4 shall be accountable for establishing and administering conformance-testing packages, including tools, test cases, test data, policies and procedures for their respective C3 Standards. If required, formal conformance testing programmes might also be established. 4 A senior committee that has the remit to validate standardization objectives or standardization proposals, to establish the related standardization tasks and to produce, endorse and maintain the resulting NATO standardization documents (AAP-15; AAP-06; AAP-42)

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 19/800 -->
## Annex 6 - Federation of Communications Services Policy
### FEDERATION OF COMMUNICATIONS SERVICES POLICY

### Background

1. Networks traditionally have a well-established coverage and capacity within the responsibility of a single service provider, such as a Nation or NATO Body.

2. A federated network is a network in which multiple service providers agree to interconnect and operate on a coordinated basis using common management arrangements and service interfaces.

3. Federating national and NATO networks increases survivability and resiliency and achieves savings and efficiencies. The federation of national and NATO networks will also help to develop mutual trust, enhance interoperability and improve the standardisation process, especially where it concerns standardised information exchange and sharing.

### Aim

4. The aim of this policy is to establish the principles for federating networks among NATO and national service providers to support NATO processes, tasks or missions.

### Scope

5. This policy addresses the establishment of federated networks by NATO Bodies and Nations.

6. The principles are applicable to NATO Bodies and NATO Nations that wish to interconnect their networks through a federated network. It is equally applicable to non- NATO Nations and International Organisations that want to interconnect their networks to a NATO-governed federated network, subject to Allies’ approval and in accordance with extant NATO Security procedures.

### Principles

7. The Alliance shall be supported by federated networks, both static and deployed, to execute its business processes, tasks and missions. These networks shall provide the required network capabilities and services to include communications transport services.

8. Federated networks shall be established using a set of flexible and tailored nonmaterial and material contributions from NATO Bodies, NATO Nations and, where applicable, non-NATO Nations and International Organisations.

9. Federated networks should support the interconnection of network elements to facilitate the extension of NATO or national services to remote NATO or national sites in line with agreed requirements of Allies and NATO. The arrangements to resource and manage any of these extensions is to be agreed on a case-by-case basis between the national and NATO service providers before any implementation.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 20/800 -->
10. The extension of communications transport services from NATO to a Nation1 or from a Nation to NATO shall at all times be governed through a Service Level Agreement.

11. A Service Level Agreement to extend NATO services over National Defence Networks shall be based on a formal requirement approved by the NATO Body or committee responsible for the supported NATO Service Customer. The consideration of eligibility and affordability of those services shall be addressed by the resource committees in line with the extant resource policies.

12. The basic principle for extending communications transport services from NATO to Nations and from Nations to NATO shall be the reciprocity principle. If the level of effort between the service providers is imbalanced, a cost-reimbursement may be considered by either provider. Common-funded financial consequences will at all times be governed by the Resource Committees.

13. The interconnection points between NATO and national networks shall support the information exchange requirements and include mediation and boundary protection functions.

14. NATO shall provide up to two network interconnection points within each NATO Nation between the NATO and national Secret networks, the second interconnection point being provided for resilience. Subject to agreed operational requirements, additional interconnection points, between NATO and national Secret networks, may be provided. Funding of these additional interconnection points shall be borne by the entity requesting the additional interconnection point.

15. The interconnection points shall be implemented in existing NATO communications sites within the Nation. These NATO communications sites may be existing NATO HQs elements, parts of the NATO Command Structure or Points of Presence. In the absence of NATO HQ elements or of NATO Command Structure entities, the location of the interconnection point shall be decided between the responsible NATO service provider and the concerned Nation.

16. The interconnection point is a demarcation point for service management and security between the NATO service provider and the external service provider, with each having full responsibility and covering the costs for its side of the connection.

17. Federated network service providers will establish Federated Network Instructions for interconnection points between member networks. These instructions shall contain the Service Interoperability Profiles that specify the characteristics of the service interfaces implemented in the interconnection points. These Service Interoperability Profiles shall be based on agreed NATO STANAGs and/or civil, commercial, or national standards and specifications. These Communications profiles shall be included within the NATO Interoperability Standards and Profiles (NISP) and be updated as required.

**Footnotes:**

1. Reciprocity within the context of this policy refers to the exchange of services between NATO and the respective Nations, without any cost reimbursement, assuming that the level of effort to provide these services to one another is similar. This implies that NATO could make use of national networks to reach users in Nations and Nations can make use of the NATO network to reach national users in the NATO network or use the NATO network as a Transit network to reach users on other Nations’ networks. This reciprocal exchange of services would be without cost-reimbursements but at all times governed by SLAs. All agreements would be considered on a case-by-case basis.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 21/800 -->
18. Service Interoperability Profiles should be considered in the NATO Defence Planning Process’ Common and Individual Targets.

19. Service Interoperability Profiles shall be supported by Concepts of Employment to facilitate implementation.

20. Changes to Service Interoperability Profiles with affected service providers will be coordinated through Service Transition Plans.

21. When a national network is connected to a NATO network, the minimum requirements for CIS Security (including Cyber defence) on the national network shall be implemented in line with PO(2014)0801, Minimum Requirements of CIS Security (Including Cyber Defence) for National CIS Critical for NATO Core Tasks.

### Roles and Responsibilities

22. NATO will fund the implementation of the services and infrastructure only to the point of the interconnection points in the Nations to meet NATO’s requirements, provided that these requirements are eligible for common funding in accordance with the extant resource policies.

23. As the primary NATO Service Provider, the NATO Communications and Information Agency (NCI Agency) is responsible for implementing the interconnection points to NATO networks. The NCI Agency will establish Federated Network Instructions for interconnection points between NATO and national networks.

24. Nations are responsible for implementing the interconnection points to their networks. Nations are responsible to fund, implement and operate any interconnection points they may require in addition to the ones provided by NATO. Nations are responsible to fund, implement and operate the transmission and connections needed to reach the NATO network interconnection points in their Nation to satisfy national requirements.

25. The C3 Board is responsible for overseeing the harmonisation of federated services and supporting interconnection standards.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 22/800 -->
Left blank intentionally

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 23/800 -->
## Annex 7 - Software Policy
### SOFTWARE POLICY

### Reference

A. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018

### Background

1. Within NATO, software management is essential to provide efficient and effective Information and Communications Technology (ICT) support for the NATO Enterprise and the Allies.

### Aim

2. The aim of this policy is to improve the quality, cost effectiveness, Alliance-wide interoperability, national use and security of NATO acquired and operated software. Software should be highly re-usable, full life cycle supported for Alliance needs.

### Scope

3. This policy applies to software, specifically developed by NATO or acquired for NATO, that is either the property of NATO or where NATO has unlimited rights to use and distribute. Such software is referred to as NATO Software1. For Alliance interoperability it is recommended that Nations apply this policy for multinational acquisitions.

### Principles

4. It is NATO policy to apply the following specific principles to the governance and management of NATO Software in order to achieve the vision of the Alliance C3 Strategy (Ref. A).

5. Acquisition of Software. For the acquisition of NATO Software the strategic principle of Adopt, Buy, Create (ABC) found in Ref. (A) shall be applied. The appropriate acquisition approach shall be determined by a business case covering the entire life-cycle. For adoption of existing software as NATO Software, it shall either become the property of NATO or NATO receives unlimited rights to use and distribute.

6. Use of NATO Software. Allies having participated in the funding of the software shall have the ability to use NATO Software on a no-fee basis, and are authorized to make NATO Software available as a service2, in line with NATO security rules, to relevant non-NATO

**Footnotes:**

1. This excludes specific hardware dependent software, such as firmware.

2. A software service is the capability provided to the consumer using the provider’s applications running on provider controlled infrastructure.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 24/800 -->
entities3 for the national purposes of respective NATO Nations, provided control remains with that NATO Nation. Software support is subject to separate agreements and funding arrangements. The provisioning of NATO Software does not automatically entitle the receiving entity to provide NATO Software, in source code or executable form, to third parties or to receive support on that NATO Software from NATO entities.

7. Support for mission critical use. In the absence of NATO Security Investment Programme (NSIP) solutions, prototype NATO Software designed for eventual fielding may be used to support urgent operational requirements provided the need to minimize operational risk outweighs the limitations of the software in terms of support or functionality.

8. Managed Software Life-cycle. NATO Software shall be developed and maintained, including appropriate funding, through an appropriate formalised life cycle methodology4. The governance over NATO Software is executed by Allies. This includes authority of Configuration Control of NATO Software baselines, which may be delegated. Life Cycle Management of each NATO Software product, including design, change and configuration management, shall be the responsibility of a single NATO body5. Where there are consequences for interoperability and software compliance with the C3 Taxonomy, architectures and agreed standards, issues shall be escalated to the configuration control authority.

9. Single NATO Software baseline. A single baseline for each NATO Software product shall be maintained and is independent of the type of deployment; this includes integration with national systems, distribution in an executable form or provision as a service.

10. Managed requirements. NATO Software shall be developed based on managed requirements, maximising opportunities to consolidate across the Alliance.

11. Integration of national requirements into NATO Software. NATO Software may address national requirements with appropriate funding arrangements and transfer of property for the results to the Alliance. Integration of national requirements is subject to approval in accordance with NAC approved regulations.

12. Consideration of security. NATO Software is subject to security risk assessment. Security mechanisms and controls shall be embedded in the life cycle of NATO Software, including its design, its use and release6,7,8.

**Footnotes:**

3. In line with Non-NATO Entities (NNEs) that are considered as the entity types established through the C-M(2012)0096, FMN Concept, 30 November 2012 and related documents.

4. NATO already has a lifecycle model for systems applied in NATO armament programmes, see C-M(2005)0108, NATO Policy For Systems Life Cycle Management, 30 November 2005.

5. Life-cycle management for different software products may be assigned to different bodies.

6. Annex 14, NATO Information Management Policy, 15 June 2015.

7. C-M(2002)49-COR12, Security Within NATO, 14 September 2015.

8. C-M(2002)60, The Management of Non-Classified NATO Information, 11 July 2002.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 25/800 -->
13. Minimize dependency on proprietary solutions. NATO Software design shall seek to minimise ‘lock-in’ to proprietary solutions. If appropriate, Open Source Software9 shall be adopted, provided adequate lifecycle support is available.

14. Evolutionary and continual development. NATO Software shall be developed in an evolutionary and incremental way in order to deliver in a timely and cost effective manner, manage risk, respond to changing requirements and leverage technological possibilities10.

15. Software Adaptability. NATO Software shall be designed to be configurable for deployment in different missions, exercises and training environments. NATO Software shall also be designed in a modular fashion with reusable software components. Awareness of such re-usable components shall be promoted.

16. Software Re-use. The acquisition of NATO Software shall optimise the re-use of software products and components.

17. Licensing of NATO Software. NATO Software shall only be used after NATO has granted an appropriate license.

18. Disclosure and provisioning of NATO Software to non-NATO entities. Disclosure and provisioning of NATO Software shall be subject to approval by Allies, on a case-by-case basis, in accordance with the procedure detailed in a separate Directive.

19. Use of NATO Software by non-NATO entities.11 The use of NATO Software by non-NATO entities shall be regulated on fair and reasonable terms and conditions and in respect of the Intellectual Property Rights owned by NATO.

20. Community Development. Allies shall be able to voluntarily contribute source code and other development effort free of charge to the further improvement of NATO Software, as long as such contribution becomes collective property of the Alliance.

### Roles and Responsibilities

21. The Consultation, Command and Control Board has:

21.1. on behalf of the North Atlantic Council, the authority to approve the release of NATO Software to non-NATO entities after consultation with other relevant senior policy committees;

21.2. accountability for monitoring compliance to this policy and supporting Directives by NATO civil and military bodies;

21.3. responsibility for resolving issues with other relevant senior policy committees where NATO Software does not comply with NATO policies and directives;

**Footnotes:**

9. Open Source Software (OSS): Software that is available to the general public where the copyright holder provides the rights to use, study, change, and improve its design through availability of its source code and distribute the software to anyone and for any purpose. OSS is often developed by a community in a public, collaborative manner.

10. As an example, virtualization.

11. A symbolic license fee sufficient to cover the costs that will be incurred in dealing with non-NATO entities, may be charged.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 26/800 -->
21.4. responsibility for ensuring that a central NATO Software Catalogue is maintained;

21.5. responsibility for issuing Directives supporting this policy.

22. The Heads of NATO civil and military bodies are responsible to:

22.1. ensure that this policy, related policies and supporting directives are implemented within their organization and shall report non-compliance to the C3 Board; In a consumer role:

22.2. authorize the use of prototype NATO Software in support of urgent operational requirements and inform the C3 Board;

22.3. elevate to the C3 Board non-conformance of NATO Software against NATO interoperability requirements; In a provider role:

22.4. ensure the timely updating of the NATO Software Catalogue for all NATO Software products and components for which they have Life Cycle Management responsibility;

22.5. support all requirement stakeholders in the management of through-life requirements of NATO Software for which they assume or have Life Cycle Management responsibility;

22.6. elevate issues to the C3 Board, or its delegated authority, when NATO Software is unable to comply with C3 policies, the C3 Taxonomy, architectures or NATO agreed interoperability standards.

22.7. National Authorities are responsible to ensure that this policy, related policies and supporting directives are implemented when using NATO Software for national purposes.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 27/800 -->
## Annex 8 - C3 Capabilities Implementation Policy
### C3 CAPABILITIES IMPLEMENTATION POLICY

### Background

1. The development, implementation, use and evolution of integrated NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services calls for the rapid adoption of mature, but not yet obsolete, commercial technologies and for the reduction in delivery time of these capabilities.

2. With the C3 Strategy1 the North Atlantic Council (NAC) approved the adoption of the Adopt, Buy, Create (ABC) concept for the procurement of C3 capabilities and ICT Services. The intent of the ABC concept is to encourage use of existing solutions in lieu of developing new ones wherever feasible. This policy elaborates this concept, encouraging the reuse of existing technology, applications and services, wherever feasible. The expected result will be reduced cost, reduced risk and reduced time spent on development and implementation of solutions.

### Aim

3. This policy aims to reduce delivery time, risk and lifecycle costs while preserving, or enhancing, performance and interoperability of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

4. This policy provides the principles to facilitate the development, evaluation, selection and implementation of proposals to address C3 Capabilities and ICT Services requirements, for the acquisition of the most effective and timely solutions.

### Scope

5. The scope of this policy covers the full spectrum of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services that will be used by NATO Enterprise Organisations2.

6. This policy is mandatory for NATO Enterprise entities that participate in the provision of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

7. This policy does not suggest changes to current processes for NATO capability implementation, ICT services provision or procurement. It is offered to the various NATO governing bodies (i.e. boards and committees), involved in the provision of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services, to guide their informed discussions and decision making.

8. Should a conflict arise between this policy and other policies, an assessment must be conducted by the C3 Board in coordination with relevant governing bodies and organisations of the NATO Enterprise to establish which of the conflicting policy principles should take precedence.

**Footnotes:**

1. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018

2. C-M(2014)0061, NATO Enterprise Approach for the Delivery of C3 Capabilities and the Provision of ICT Services, 14 November 2014

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 28/800 -->
### Principles

9. Investigation of Alternatives. Alternatives shall be investigated to include: use of existing Services, adoption of NATO solutions, adoption of National solutions, purchasing of COTS, outsourcing, creating new capabilities or any combination of the above. Recognizing that not all situations will be the same, there is no prescribed order in the preference of options considered.

10. Shaping of Requirements to Fit Existing Solutions. Requirement shall be shaped to fit existing solutions where possible or a trade-off shall be proposed between meeting 100% of the original requirements and the advantages of reducing risk and decreasing time to implementation and operation.

11. Business Case Recommendations. A business case shall be developed, to be included in the Type B Cost Estimate (TBCE) that outlines the potential alternative approaches (adopt, buy, create or some combination) and makes a recommendation to the governing body on the implementation.

12. Testing and Accreditation. The chosen alternative must have been tested and accredited or have a clear path to being tested and accredited by NATO.

13. Modular Design. If the create option is necessary the solution shall be built in a modular fashion to allow complete or partial reuse by other NATO Enterprise organizations and Nations.

14. Termination of Obsolete Technology. To avoid inextricably linking financial resources to an approved investment, where scant progress is made in the face of an aging requirement, early termination of a procurement initiative shall be considered. Considerations shall also be made on optimizing the time for introducing new technology to succeed aging technology in a Life Cycle Cost-perspective.

### Roles and Responsibilities

15. Host nations and service providers shall investigate alternatives to address C3 requirements.

16. The Host Nation or the Service Provider shall interact iteratively with the requirement holders to shape the requirements to fit existing solutions, where possible.

17. The Host Nation or the Service Provider shall propose, to the appropriate decision governance body, the trade-off between meeting 100% of the original requirements and the advantages of reducing risk as well as decreasing time to implementation and operation.

18. Host Nation or the service provider will develop a business case to be included in the Type B Cost Estimate (TBCE).

19. The requirement holders and/or the service providers shall develop a proposal for the early termination of a procurement initiative, as appropriate, including also consideration for optimising the introduction of new technology, from a Life Cycle Cost-perspective.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 29/800 -->
## Annex 9 - Enterprise Architecture Policy
### ENTERPRISE ARCHITECTURE POLICY

### Background

1. The NATO Enterprise Approach1 describes the requirement for standardized NATO C3 capabilities and interoperable ICT services for the fulfilment of NATO’s goals and objectives and for the conduct of NATO’s daily business process activities, operation, training and exercises.

2. The Alliance C3 Strategy2 explicitly calls for a NATO Enterprise Architecture (EA) discipline.

### Scope

3. This policy establishes the key objectives and principles for NATO’s Enterprise Architecture discipline, identifies common architecture types and re-usable components as well as NATO EA-related roles and responsibilities.

4. This policy is mandatory for all entities involved in the definition and implementation of static and deployable C3 Capabilities and ICT Services for the NATO Enterprise, and is recommended for implementation in NATO Nations and by federation partners. Objectives

5. The key objectives of NATO’s Enterprise Architecture discipline are to:

5.1. translate NATO’s vision and strategy into effective change of the current business and IT environments, and support the initiatives required to migrate to the desired future state;

5.2. support the effective and efficient development (identification, specification, implementation, verification and validation) of interoperable capabilities for the NATO Enterprise;

5.3. enable the re-use of these capabilities in an Alliance and Federation context; and

5.4. facilitate governance and management activities at the portfolio3, programme4 and project5 level.

**Footnotes:**

1. C-M(2014)0061, NATO Enterprise Approach for the Delivery of C3 Capabilities and the Provision of ICT Services, 14 November 2014

2. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018.

3. The totality of the change initiatives within an organization; it may comprise a number of programmes, standalone projects and other initiatives that achieve congruence of change.

4. A temporary flexible organization structure created to coordinate, direct and oversee the implementation of a set of related projects and activities in order to deliver outcomes and benefits related to the organization’s strategic objectives. A programme is likely to have a life that spans several years.

5. A temporary organization that is created for the purpose of delivering one or more business products according to an agreed business case.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 30/800 -->
### Principles

6. Architecture support for strategy definition. NATO Enterprise entities shall use enterprise architectures to support the definition of business and ICT strategies, and to assess the complexity, feasibility, cost and dependencies of strategic change initiatives.

7. Architecture support for strategy execution. NATO Enterprise entities shall use enterprise architectures to support the execution of business and ICT strategies. To this end, enterprise architectures shall be embedded into relevant processes such as resource planning, programme / project management requirements management or acquisition and procurement.

8. Requirements-based change. NATO Enterprise entities shall use enterprise architectures to ensure that changes to applications and technology are solely made in response to business needs. Changes will only be made following examination of the proposed change against the enterprise architectures. Solution requirements described by architectural products shall maintain traceability with operational requirements.

9. Standardized architecture deliverables. NATO Enterprise entities shall develop architectures using standardized deliverables to increase understandability. The deliverables shall, wherever possible, be developed using the content meta model and views described in the NATO Architecture Framework (NAF).

10. Re-use, and traceability, of architecture content. In order to define and implement C3 capabilities and ICT services in an effective, efficient and consistent manner across the NATO Enterprise, architecture deliverables of lower level architectures shall reuse the content of, or shall be traceable to, deliverables of higher level architectures. This implies that, e.g. architecture content at the capability level shall be traceable to content on the enterprise level (e.g. the C3 Taxonomy6, the NATO Interoperability Standards and Profiles or the C3 Integrated Master Plan).

11. Standardized architecture process. NATO Enterprise entities shall develop and use architectures following a standardized process to increase collaboration and understanding between different entities. The process shall be based on the Architecture Development Method described in The Open Group Architecture Framework (TOGAF)7.

12. Sharing of NATO Enterprise Architecture Information. NATO Enterprise Architecture Information, describing artefacts at the appropriate level (e.g. capability level, cfr. paragraph 16), shall be accessible to interested parties by means of a shared solution to facilitate the understanding of the interrelationship between artefacts at the same architecture level and across levels.

13. Architecture as a Service. Architecture as a service shall to be supported by a comprehensive Service design for the definition of operational services to link ICT services with operational services.

14. Adoption of the C3 Taxonomy. NATO common funded C3 Capabilities shall apply the latest approved version of the C3 Taxonomy to uniquely specify service interoperability requirements at the interfaces between NATO and National systems.

**Footnotes:**

6. AC/322-D(2016)0017, C3 Taxonomy Baseline 2.0, 14 March 2016

7. TOGAF is an industry standard for architecture development maintained and published by the Open Group (http://pubs.opengroup.org/architecture/togaf9-doc/arch & www.opengroup.org)

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 31/800 -->
15. Nations are also encouraged to apply the C3 Taxonomy to facilitate the achievement of interoperability between NATO and National capability components at the technical services level.

### Architecture Levels And Types

14. In order to manage the complexity of the NATO Enterprise, NATO maintains architectures at three levels8 as defined below.

15. Architectures at the NATO Enterprise level9. They provide the architecture content that form the foundation for architectural coherence across the entire NATO Enterprise, and beyond. Architectures at this level will span many change programmes (e.g. capability packages). Key components of architectures at the NATO Enterprise-level are:

15.1. The C3 Taxonomy and the Technical Services Framework that provide a common language for the description of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services;

15.2. The C3 Integrated Master Plan (IMP) which provides an overview of the status of key C3 Capabilities; and

15.3. The NATO Interoperability Standards and Profiles (NISP) which provides the most current baseline of interoperability profiles and technical standards.

16. Architectures at the capability level10. Architectures at this level are used to support the delivery of large, multi-phased and multi-project change initiatives (e.g. capability packages). They are used to:

16.1. facilitate the definition of resource proposals to address existing shortfalls between the available (As-Is) capability and the required (To-Be) capability;

16.2. support the scoping and phasing of projects to resolve these shortfalls;

16.3. identify synergies between the projects; and

16.4. direct, monitor and evaluate the execution of a set of related projects.

17. Architectures at the project level11. Architectures at this level are describing the envisioned solution that the project aims to implement. They are used to:

17.1. articulate the high-level design for a project in order to govern its execution;

17.2. assure compliance with architectural governance;

17.3. support the integration and alignment between projects;

17.4. inform budget and acquisition decisions (e.g. in a Type B Cost Estimate (TBCE));

17.5. provide the basis for more detailed specifications during contract preparation (e.g. in an Invitation for Bids (IFB));

17.6. support contractor selection;

17.7. govern the delivery of contracted products and services;

**Footnotes:**

8. Architectures on all levels can describe an As-Is (baseline) state and a To-Be (target) state.

9. Previously known as Overarching Architecture.

10. Previously known as Reference Architecture.

11. Previously known as Target Architecture.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 32/800 -->
17.8. provide the basis for service transition and service operation activities; and

17.9. support traceability between user requirements and solutions.

18. Architectures on each level shall cover the following architecture domains/types:

18.1. Business Architecture – describing the business strategy, management, organization, and key business processes (including process ownership and key decisions) of the organization;

18.2. Information Architecture – describing the structure of an organization's logical and physical information assets and the associated data management resources and linking the information required to the key business processes and decisions;

18.3. Application Architecture – providing a blueprint for the individual application systems to be deployed, the information which they provide, the interactions between the application systems and their relationships to the core business processes of the organization with the frameworks for services to be exposed as business functions for integration; and

18.4. Technology Architecture – describing the hardware, software and network infrastructure needed to support the deployment of the application systems.

### Roles and Responsibilities

19. NATO’s EA discipline can only be successful when it is embedded in, and supportive to all NATO processes dealing with capability definition and implementation. The following roles and responsibilities are defined to cover the complete range of governance and management activities in capability definition and implementation within NATO.

20. The Senior Policy Committees shall ensure the implementation of this policy in observance of the C3 Policy Compliance Framework12.

21. The C3 Capability Area Manager shall steer a group of NATO Senior C3 Stakeholders, for them to:

21.1. ensure that NATO EA methods and deliverables are embedded in relevant processes for capability definition and implementation;

21.2. coordinate architecture development and use at the NATO Enterprise level and oversee these activities at capability level;

21.3. orchestrate the resolution of high-level NATO EA issues; and

21.4. promote cohesion of the NATO EA process with national and industry standard architecture processes.

22. The Heads of NATO Enterprise entities shall:

22.1. use architecture actively in the development and implementation of C3 capabilities and ICT services, in order to support NATO’s transition towards a more mature Enterprise Architecture discipline;

22.2. implement organizational structures accountable for the implementation of this policy and supporting directives and related training and education activities;

**Footnotes:**

12. C-M(2016)0011.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 33/800 -->
22.3. develop appropriate directives for the implementation of this policy; and

22.4. report compliance with the principles of this policy to the relevant governing body.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 34/800 -->
Left blank intentionally

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 35/800 -->
## Annex 10 - Cloud Computing Policy
### CLOUD COMPUTING POLICY

### Background

1. The Alliance C3 Strategy1 sets a vision for Information and Communications Technology (ICT) to underpin the NATO’s Strategic Concept.

2. NATO intends to attain ICT solutions, coordinated in funding and service provisioning, through an approach that treats ICT infrastructure as a shared commodity, with a view to achieve economies of scale, reduced investment costs and O&M efforts.

3. Consistent with industry's best practices, the Alliance approved an ICT Service Management Policy2 stating “that all NATO apportioned ICT solutions shall be defined and provided as services”. The Alliance has also adopted an Enterprise Approach3 for the delivery of C3 Capabilities and the provision of ICT Services, which aims at harmonizing ICT services, driving commonality across NATO bodies and increasing effectiveness and efficiencies.

4. The adoption of a cloud computing approach is necessary for the efficient delivery of C3 Capabilities and the consumption of ICT Services. This approach delivers infrastructure, platforms and software using service models and will support the Service Management Policy and the Enterprise Approach’s ambitions.

### Aim

5. This policy introduces a new business model that treats infrastructure as a shared commodity that is accessed as a service. It sets the principles for providing, maintaining and using a shared, service-oriented computing infrastructure (cloud) to achieve greater availability, flexibility, agility, security and mobility while seeking to reduce costs.

### Scope

6. This policy outlines the principles for service providers, customers and users of the NATO Enterprise to apply a cloud computing approach that includes, inter alia:

6.1. Establishment of ICT infrastructure and common platforms as commodities;

6.2. Development, testing and deployment/hosting of new applications;

6.3. Hosting of legacy applications;

6.4. Provision of location-agnostic storage services.

**Footnotes:**

1. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018.

2. Annex 2, Information and Communications Technology Service Management Policy.

3. C-M(2014)0061, The NATO Enterprise Approach for the Delivery of C3 Capabilities and the Provision of ICT. Services, 14 November 2014.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 36/800 -->
### Principles

7. The principles in this policy are mandatory for NATO bodies and they affect all ICT solutions and services supporting the NATO Enterprise which may be NATO or nationally owned, leased or commercially provided.

8. Service Models. NATO Enterprise shall follow a cloud computing approach incorporating ICT Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS) and Software as a Service (SaaS) models.

9. ICT Infrastructure4. The goal is one ICT infrastructure5 supporting the entire NATO Enterprise for all information domains and all levels of security up to and including NATO SECRET6. This will maximise seamless information sharing and pooling of computing resource across the NATO Enterprise.

10. ICT Infrastructure Partitions. The NATO Enterprise cloud shall allow the creation of Community of Interest (COI) segments via logical/virtual partitioning. The use of such COI segments will need to be justified.

11. Deployment7 Model. A NATO Enterprise ICT infrastructure, handling NATO CONFIDENTIAL and above information, shall use a private cloud deployment model that is owned by NATO or by an Allied Nation. For the infrastructure handling information classified NATO RESTRICTED and below, community, hybrid or public deployment models may be considered once all information assurance and data sovereignty considerations are satisfied. The determination of appropriate service and deployment models for all business functions or operational requirements shall take account of operational constraints, effectiveness of ICT services, and cost efficiency.

12. Cloud Security. NATO cloud services shall comply with the NATO security policies and directives for CIS.

13. Data Protection and Sovereignty. Alliance data protection in a cloud shall ensure the ability to control the access, alteration, archiving and deletion of digital information. This control may be limited by different aspects like laws of the country in which the data is stored or policies of the service providers involved.

14. Cloud-First. All future ICT solutions within the NATO Enterprise should use the NATO Enterprise’s cloud infrastructure rather than deploying distinct additional infrastructures.

15. Cloud Services Request. All requests for cloud services by NATO entities must be addressed to the service provider.

16. Re-Use. When considering the re-use of ICT solutions, care must be taken to ensure the re-used capability can be re-hosted in the cloud computing environment.

**Footnotes:**

4. An ICT infrastructure refers to all the composite hardware, software, networking resources to allow an organization to deliver ICT solutions to its users.

5. Until appropriate technologies can be employed, physically distinct infrastructures will be required to host services at different classification levels. Therefore, infrastructure shall in principle be rationalized to one Enterprise-wide infrastructure up to and including NATO SECRET and one Enterprise-wide infrastructure up to and including NATO RESTRICTED, the latter with connection to and through the Internet.

6. ICT solutions above NATO SECRET are not addressed in this policy.

7. In the context of cloud computing, deployment refers to ICT solutions.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 37/800 -->
17. Legacy Migration. Legacy ICT solutions will be migrated to the cloud computing environment only after careful analysis to ensure there is a reasonable business case.

18. Cloud Business Model.

18.1. ICT solutions will be designed with the expectation that the infrastructure has already been designed and will be provisioned, when needed.

18.2. ICT solutions will be hosted on infrastructure owned, operated and maintained by the Cloud Service Provider.

18.3. Service providers will be responsible for acquiring, running and maintaining the cloud infrastructure in accordance with the outline at Figure 1.

19. Application development shall use a shared cloud infrastructure and shall access that infrastructure through a service interface. The Service Provider shall provide a robust application development environment to allow for smooth transition between development, test and production environments.

### Roles and Responsibilities

20. In line with its mandate8, the C3 Board will provide guidance and directives for the implementation of this policy, in conjunction with other relevant Senior Political Committees.

21. Heads of NATO civil and military bodies:

21.1. In a service user role, are responsible to identify requirements for business and operational functions to utilise cloud computing.

21.2. In a service customer role, shall provide useful inputs for the revision of NATO policy and practices in the appropriate domains, with a view to promote the use of the cloud computing approach.

22. Service providers shall enable provision of cloud computing by:

22.1. Brokering acquisition of cloud services;

22.2. Migrating eligible Enterprise services to cloud computing;

22.3. Ensuring cloud services interoperability based on NATO and open standards;

22.4. Providing ample description of all service interfaces to allow third party developers to build additional services and applications;

22.5. Assuming responsibility for the operations, maintenance and security of the cloud infrastructure.

**Footnotes:**

8. C-M(2012)0079, Mandate of the Consultation, Command and Control (C3) Board, 10 September 2012.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 38/800 -->
<!-- Original visible header: ANNEX 10; C-M(2015)0041-REV2; original page marker 10-4 -->
**Figure 1 - Cloud Stack Model**
> 图像说明：Figure 1 展示 Cloud Stack Model。图上方按列标出 Service Models、Cloud Stack、Stack Component 和 Who is Responsible。左侧 Service Models 以纵向区域表示 SaaS、PaaS、IaaS；中间 Cloud Stack 自上而下包含 User、Application、Application Stack、Infrastructure 四层。User 层包含 Login、Registration、User Admin；Application 层包含 Business Processes、User Interface、Reports、Transactions；Application Stack 层包含 Programming Languages、App Server、Data Base、Web Server、Orchestration、Middleware；Infrastructure 层包含 Data Center、Networking、Virtual Machines、Hypervisor、Disk Storage、OS、Firewall、Load Balancing。右侧责任区域以纵向条显示 Capability Owner、Service Provider 等责任主体，不同服务模型下责任边界随云栈层级变化。

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 39/800 -->
## Annex 11 - Green IT Policy
### GREEN1 IT POLICY

### References

A. PO(2014)0059, Green Defence Framework, 11 Feb 2014

B. C-M(2002)49, Security within the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), 17 Jun 2002

C. C-M(2002)60, The Management of Non-Classified NATO Information, 11 Jul 2002

### Background

1. The 2010 Strategic Concept2 identifies that “key environmental and resource constraints […] and increasing energy needs will further shape the future security environment in areas of concern to NATO and have the potential to significantly affect NATO planning and operations”.

2. At the 2012 Chicago Summit3, NATO Heads of State and Government (HoSG) committed to “work towards significantly improving the energy efficiency of our military forces”.

3. The NAC-agreed Green Defence Framework (Ref. Error! Reference source not found.), which was noted by the HoSG at the 2014 Wales Summit, recognises that:

4. Green defence activities “could potentially reduce cost and lower the risks to Allied soldiers and help reducing the environmental footprint”;

4.1. “Increased energy efficiency responds to environmental concerns in Allies’ public opinion and demonstrates that NATO is responsive to them”;

4.2. “Saving energy and demonstrating environmental awareness are enduring benefits. […] this coherent Green Defence framework will contribute to the “green” profile of NATO”.

4.3. The Council (Ref. Error! Reference source not found.) tasks “NATO bodies and committees, including the NATO Military Authorities, to continue mainstreaming Green Defence efforts in their respective strands of work […] taking into account the Green Defence framework”.

5. With respect to Information Technology (IT) the Council (Ref. Error! Reference source not found.) agrees that “NATO could consider the applicability of “green” standards and principles across the NATO HQ, NATO Command Structure and NATO Agencies, and consider the applicability of setting up “green” accounting and benchmarks to measure progress”.

**Footnotes:**

1. The term “green” is used throughout the document in compliance with previous agreed NATO language, particularly in Ref. A. “Green IT” refers to the application of environmentally sustainable policies across the life-cycle of IT systems and services within the NATO Enterprise.

2. Strategic Concept for the Defence and Security of the Members of NATO, 20 November 2010

3. Chicago Summit Declaration, 20 May 2012

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 40/800 -->
### Aim

6. This Policy provides principles on defining, acquiring, operating and disposing of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services in an environmentally sustainable manner.

### Scope

7. The scope of this Policy covers the full spectrum of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services used within the NATO Enterprise4.

8. This Policy is mandatory for all NATO Enterprise entities.

### Principles

9. Green IT principles shall enable business processes to become more energy efficient and environmentally sustainable, and promote NATO’s “green” profile, in accordance with the aims of the Green Defence Framework.

10. Green IT principles shall be applicable to all stages of the lifecycle for C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

11. Applied Green IT principles shall preserve the effectiveness, interoperability, security and resilience of the C3 Capabilities and ICT Services. Capabilities Definition

12. When developing C3 capabilities, IT assets shall be properly tailored to the requirements in order to avoid excessive allocation of IT resources to individual users. At the capability level, notwithstanding the need to cater for flexibility and dynamic growth, the IT capacity shall not be over-provisioned above an agreed target, specific to each capability. Acquisition and Introduction in Service

13. Green standards and best practices. C3 Capabilities and ICT services shall be acquired and introduced in service by utilising energy-efficient and green manufacturing standards, and in full consideration of industry best-practices5. This should include considerations of sustainable supply chains.

14. A cost/benefit analysis of the lifecycle costs of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services using energy-efficient standards shall be performed during the acquisition process. Due considerations to the lifecycle environmental impact shall also be part of this analysis.

**Footnotes:**

4. AC/322-D(2015)0014-REV3, The NATO Enterprise Approach for the Delivery of C3 Capabilities and the Provision of ICT Services, 11 December 2015

5. Examples: The US ENERGY STAR certification (the EU is also involved); the EU Ecodesign Directive (2009/125/EC); the new EU Environmental Footprint (EF) approach, both for products (PEF) and organisations (OEF), currently under testing.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 41/800 -->
Usage – ICT as an Enabler to reduce overall resource consumption

15. Optimal use for energy efficiency. Service providers and service users shall ensure the use of energy efficiency mechanisms embedded within C3 Capabilities by aiming at agreed levels of consumption.

16. Monitoring. The energy consumption and the environmental impact shall be monitored. This is essential to ensure that the energy is used efficiently and that the environmental footprint is systematically reviewed.

17. Paperless environment. NATO Enterprise shall continue to exploit the potential of ICT services with a view to achieving a paperless environment by 2025. NATO Enterprise entities shall adopt paper use reduction plans which will include yearly targets.

18. Collaboration tools. The capacity and availability of Collaboration tools shall be enhanced and exploited to the maximum extent possible in order to adapt NATO business processes, thereby resulting in the reduction of resource-intensive activities (e.g. business travel for meetings)

19. Use of virtualisation. As a matter of priority, the service customers shall consider and the service providers shall implement virtualisation solutions, in compliance with NATO policies (Ref. B, C).

20. Staff training and awareness. Staffs shall be provided with adequate training and awareness on best practices with regards to the efficient, environmentally use of IT. Disposal

21. Components re-use/recycling. Components of electronic waste shall be re-used or recycled in accordance with the applicable National or international legislation, whilst considering the NATO Security Policy (Ref. B) to ensure proper disposal of classified information and components before recycling.

22. Consistent management of hazardous material. Hazardous waste shall be consistently identified as such and subsequently handled in accordance with the applicable National or international legislation.

### Roles and Responsibilities

23. The Consultation, Command and Control Board (C3B):

23.1. Shall oversee the development and maintenance of a Green IT Policy implementation Directive (including, but not limited to: references to NATO/international standards, quantitative and qualitative targets (where feasible) and compliance mechanisms), in consultation with other relevant Senior Policy Committees;

23.2. Shall monitor the implementation of the Policy and Directive.

24. Relevant Senior Policy Committees:

24.1. Shall ensure the implementation of this Policy, in the context of the NATO Green Defence Framework;

24.2. Shall contribute to the development and update of the Green IT Directive.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 42/800 -->
25. Heads of NATO Enterprise entities shall ensure the implementation of this Policy and the subsequent Directive, and shall conform to a compliance mechanism.

26. The Host Nation or Service Provider shall follow this policy and related directives/guidance in the process of acquisition (adopt, buy, create), introduction in service, and disposal of the C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 43/800 -->
## Annex 12 - Internet Protocol Version 6 (IPv6) Policy
INTERNET PROTOCOL VERSION 6 (IPv6) POLICY

### Background

1. Internet Protocol (IP) networks largely use IP version 4 (IPv4) addresses. Although this seemed initially to provide an unlimited number of addresses, the increase in IP devices, and combined with their required continuous connectivity caused a shortage of IPv4 address space. As the address space was foreseen to be depleted, IP version 6 (IPv6) was developed offering a larger address space with a virtually unlimited number of IPv6 addresses. Consequently, IPv6 offers a number of advantages in terms of IP addressing efficiencies and proponents advertise additional advantages through improvements in mobility, security and management.

2. There is, within the NATO networks, not being part of the Internet, no shortage of IPv4 address space. As such, there no single overriding technical reason that by itself forces NATO to move to IPv6. For example, NATO utilizes the whole IPv4 address space within its closed networks, while also Network Address Translation (NAT) is implemented. Nevertheless, the need to stay in line with commercial developments and considering the military operational benefits (increase in ease of management), NATO and nations are committed to migrate to IPv6. Consequently, as NATO and national networks become increasingly federated, the plans to migrate to IPv6 need to be aligned and harmonised. In particular, Industry and service providers are starting to reduce efforts in further development of IPv4-based solutions in favour of IPv6, and are increasingly transitioning to IPv6 capabilities1.

### Aim

3. This policy provides the principles for a smooth and gradual migration towards IPv6 in order to harmonise the migration to IPv6, in alignment with Industry and Service Providers, of the NATO Enterprise.

### Scope

4. The scope of this Policy covers the NATO Common Funded C3 Capabilities and ICT Services.

5. This Policy is mandatory for the NATO Enterprise, and NATO nations are encouraged to use this policy as a basis for their national IPv6 migration.

**Footnotes:**

1. This is further amplified in a statement by the Internet Architecture Board (https://www.iab.org/2016/11/07/iab-statement-on-ipv6/) and through RFC 6540 that considers it a current best practice to consider IPv6 no longer optional.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 44/800 -->
### Principles

6. The migration to IPv6 will be driven by commercial developments as well as operational requirements and these will determine the timelines for a full implementation of IPv6.

7. Although the end-state is an IPv6-only environment, the migration to IPv6 will not occur at a single moment, IPv4 and IPv6 will co-exist for some time. This co-existence should be as limited as possible to avoid spending additional resources by supporting both IPv4 and IPv6.

8. Migration to IPv6 should not result in any degradation of service capabilities, therefore it should not occur until mature IPv6 capabilities exist equivalent to those of IPv4

9. New projects and major upgrades to existing capabilities of the extant C3 capabilities (hardware and software to include applications) should be dual-stack IPv4/IPv6 capable and be able to communicate over IPv4 only and IPv6 only networks.

10. Existing and legacy C3 Capabilities can use other than dual-stack approaches to transition from IPv4 to IPv6. The choice for other transition strategies shall be based on security, interoperability, performance and cost considerations.

11. Prior to the implementation of IPv6, whether in IPv6-only or in IPv4/IPv6-mode, a vulnerability assessment shall be conducted before the networks, that include IPv6, carry any operational traffic.

12. New bespoke applications, whether procured or developed, shall have both IPv4 and IPv6 software interfaces for data transfer and name-to-address resolution.

13. During the transition period, guided by the IPv6 transition strategy and plan, IPv6 enabled C3 Capabilities shall also be accessible from IPv4 only C3 clients and networks.

14. The interworking between the IPv4 and IPv6 versions of software shall be tested prior to deployment of the software, and verified and validated to support the full functional and non-functional requirements, including information assurance, across the interworking.

15. The migration to IPv6 shall be guided by an overarching IPv6 transition strategy and plan. This plan shall include target dates for the complete migration to an IPv6-Only environment.

16. The implementation status of IPv6 shall be developed and maintained.

17. An address and naming plan for NATO and the Alliance as a whole shall be defined to include federated networks using the globally unique address space as assigned by the International Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA).

### Roles and Responsibilities

18. The Consultation, Command and Control Board (C3B) shall oversee the development of an overarching IPv6 migration strategy and implementation plan.

19. The NCI Agency shall develop an IPv6 migration strategy and Implementation Plan.

20. The NCI Agency shall ensure the implementation of this Policy and define and IPv6 address and naming plan.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 45/800 -->
## Annex 13 - Data Management Policy
### DATA MANAGEMENT POLICY

### Background

1. Staffs at all levels must have complete, accurate and timely information in order to plan and execute their missions and management functions. The basis for this is data, which through proper application in context becomes meaningful, valuable, relevant and useful information.

2. Data management is an enterprise business function. Data is costly to collect, communicate, process, store and maintain. As the value and cost of data increases, it becomes important that this critical resource is managed in a manner similar to other corporate resources.

3. The handling of high volumes of structured and unstructured data, coming with high velocity from a large variety of human and machine sources, requires adequate analytics and management tools to complement the more traditional business intelligence approach.

4. Modern tools allow for the integration of high-quality metadata to enable search, discovery, sharing and in general intelligent use and preservation of data and information, that should be used as enabler for effective data management.

5. This policy is harmonised with and directly supports the NATO Information and Knowledge Management Policy (NIMP), which constitutes the overarching framework for information management in NATO.

6. The appropriate management and exchange of data within the NATO Enterprise1, the Alliance, and the federation with other authorized partners is a prerequisite for the implementation of the Alliance C3 Strategy2. It is recommended that the principles expressed in this policy are also applied by the NATO partners when operating in a NATOled federated environment.

### Aim

7. This Policy aims to support NATO in the execution of its tasks by setting the mandatory principles for the effective and efficient management of data and metadata, with a view to enhancing adequate quality, security, integrity, protection, availability, interoperability, understanding and effective use of data in the NATO Enterprise, in the Alliance, and in coalitions with Partners.

### Scope

8. The scope of this Policy covers the NATO Enterprise, the NATO Nations and organisations, the NATO coalitions, and all non-NATO Nations and organisations when handling NATO data.

**Footnotes:**

1. The NATO Enterprise approach is defined in C-M(2014)0061, NATO Enterprise Approach, 14 November

2014.

2. C-M(2018)0037, Alliance C3 Strategy, 13 July 2018.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 46/800 -->
9. This Policy is mandatory for all NATO Enterprise entities when handling data in their custody.

### Principles

Data Management Principles

10. Data as a corporate asset. Specific processes shall be put in place for accessing, using, sharing and protecting3 data as a corporate asset. Its value is increased through the widespread and appropriate use of high quality data. Its value is diminished through misuse, misinterpretation or unnecessary access restrictions.

11. Data Visibility. Organisations shall ensure that data is advertised and can be discovered by users and applications, and that duplication of existing data is minimized.

12. Data Accessibility. Data shall be accessible in a shared networked environment to users and applications, unless policies, regulations, or other security means prohibit such access. Data repositories shall be managed, provisioned and administered across the data life-cycle in accordance with NATO policies4, standards and best practices.

13. Data Preservation. Data shall be reviewed throughout its life-cycle to determine its value. Data of permanent value shall be retained and archived, whilst data of temporary value shall be destroyed when no longer needed - in accordance with NATO policies, standards and best practices.

14. Data Interoperability. Organisations shall allow users and applications to exchange, understand and interpret data, both structurally and semantically and to determine how it may be used for their specific needs5. XML6 shall be used as a syntax for data exchange.

15. Data Assurance and Security Management. Organisations shall ensure proper authentication, authorization, integrity, access, auditing and handling of data. Users and applications shall have the ability to determine and assess the authority of the source and integrity of the data7.

16. Data Quality. Users and applications shall have the ability to measure, assess, and ensure data to be fit for purpose and fit for use.

17. Data Architectures. Organisations shall use appropriate data architectures in support to planning for the provision of high quality data, collecting of data requirements, guiding data integration, controlling data assets and aligning data investments within NATO.

**Footnotes:**

3. Implementation directive(s)/guidance shall also address issues such as the deterioration of the electronic storage media, a nuclear fallout, etc.

4. To include policies and directives related to the appraisal, retention and long-term preservation, currently available in C-M(2002)49, C-M(2002)60, C-M(2011)0043; C-M(2009)0021 and AC/324-D(2014)0008.

5. A core data framework shall be developed and maintained, in order to facilitate interoperability and the exchange of data across Communities of Interest. The framework shall contain semantic reference model(s), naming and design rules, information exchange specifications, and be enabled by standardized data mediation and integration services.

6. XML - eXtensible Markup Language.

7. Implementation directive(s)/guidance shall also address aspects such as the electronic signatures, the prevention of changes in a data chain, data labelling and binding, data viruses, etc.

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 47/800 -->
18. Data as an Enterprise Resource. Data shall be unique across the NATO Enterprise, shall be developed once and shall be coherently managed across, and available to all relevant users and communities of interest. New programs should search for existing data elements in the repositories before creating new data elements.

19. Master Data. Critical data shall be consistently defined, stored and managed to provide a single point of reference8 across the NATO Enterprise.

20. Data Stewards. Data stewards shall be established to manage the NATO Enterprise data. Metadata Management Principles

21. Metadata use. Metadata shall be defined to allow discoverability of data and information, to enable the description of the structure and the content of data and information, to provide sufficient details for authenticity, pedigree, protection, security classification, and access control, to facilitate the identification of ownership and custodianship, to facilitate retention and disposition and long-term preservation, and to support system interoperability and information exchange.

22. NATO core metadata. Any data and information exchanged on NATO systems shall be accompanied with an approved core set of metadata9.

23. Metadata schemas. Various communities of interest shall use metadata schemas that are aligned with the NATO core metadata. Metadata schemas shall be registered and permanently updated in a common repository10.

24. Metadata classification. In principle, metadata and its metadata schema should be unclassified, visible to, accessible and exchangeable by all authorised users and systems. As a consequence, metadata may have a different classification level from the data or information that it is associated with.

25. Metadata evolution and life-cycle. Metadata shall evolve and be persistently associated with the data and information throughout their life-cycle. Metadata of records shall be maintained for long-term preservation beyond the destruction of the record itself.

26. Marking, labelling and binding. Metadata shall support a coherent and standardised approach to marking, labelling and binding of metadata to data and information.

27. Automation. The capture of metadata shall be automated where possible. To ensure both human-readable and machine-readable metadata, XML shall be used as the primary language to structure metadata.

28. Data management principles applicability. Data management principles related to visibility, accessibility, interoperability, quality, assurance and security shall apply to metadata management.

**Footnotes:**

8. A trusted single version of the truth from which to base decision.

9. Implementation directive(s)/guidance shall provide details for a core set of metadata (currently the NATO Core Metadata Specification - NCMS).

10. Implementation directive(s)/guidance shall provide details for a metadata repository (currently the NATO Metadata Registry and Repository – NMRR).

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 48/800 -->
### Roles and Responsibilities

29. All individuals, communities of interest, and applications11 who create, manage, or maintain data and metadata shall apply the principles set out in this policy;

30. The Heads of NATO civil and military bodies shall ensure that this policy is applied within their organisations;

31. The C3 Board, through its delegated authority, shall:

31.1. Coordinate the actions of those NATO civil and military bodies concerning implementation of this policy;

31.2. Develop, own and maintain the NATO-wide set of standards and specifications, as well as the processes for managing NATO data and metadata;

31.3. Provide further direction and guidance.

32. National Authorities shall ensure that this policy, related policies and supporting directives and guidance are complied with when dealing with NATO data and information.

**Footnotes:**

11. For applications this responsibility falls upon the developers or the NATO bodies who acquire them through adoption or acquisition.

### 2. APP11Product

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| # | 2 |
| Name | APP11Product |
| Qualified Name | INTEL-FS Spiral 2::NATO::JISR::Staff::Product::APP-11::APP11Product |
| Type | `Type:<Class>` |
| Description | APP-11 is the definitive source of NATO agreed ADatP-3 formatted messages. The APP-11 NATO Message Catalogue provides users, system developers and Message Text Format (MTF) managers with a library of messages and instructions for their use. It is a compendium of formatted messages, structured messages, and voice templates for the exchange of information within and between NATO Forces. The use of formatted messages as contained in this catalogue is mandatory for all NATO forces exchanging character-orientated messages. APP-11 consists of all approved formatted, selected structured user formats and voice templates with supporting instructions and data tables.[APP-11-D-1] |
| Source URI | platform:/resource/INTEL-FS/INTEL-FS%20CIM%20RDM.emx#_te2tUKDHEeqz4qMNCq9PtQ |

<!-- PDF_PAGE: 105/800 -->
<!-- Original visible header retained as metadata: NAF 4.0 L7 Information Model; Data Dictionary Version 1.1; original data dictionary page marker 57 / 1798 -->

| Attribute Name | Type Name | Defining Class | Multiplicity | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Address | PhysicalAddress | ProductIIE | 0..1 |  |
| AnalystComment | String | IntelligenceInformationEntity | 0..1 | Comment on the product. The Comment field will identify analyst comments regarding the implications of the information and other assessments. [IFAS1-SOP] |
| BPs | BusinessProcess | IntelligenceInformationEntity | 0..* |  |
| BasicEncyclopediaNumber | BasicEncyclopediaNumberType | ProductIIE | 0..1 | The Basic Encyclopaedia (BE) Number is generated by a number of agencies involved in maintaining United States military databases and exists for a very large number of significant installations in the world. The AIntP-03 Key is based on a very similar data structure and can be easily converted to a BE Number. It is, however, recommended that when the BE Number for an Installation (or Facility) is known that it should be entered into the BE NUMBER Field and the BE NUMBER IS KEY Field switched to "Y" (Yes). Thus the BE Number would act as the Key for your record, improving the ability to interchange records without duplication or the creation on multiple, mismatched records on a single Place.[AINTP-3] |
| CSDSourceLibrary | String | ProductIIE | 0..1 |  |

| Description | An activity, or the occurrence of an activity, that may utilise resources and may be focused against an objective. [MIM-5.1]An Action is the process of engaging any instrument of power (Political, Military, Economic and Civil - PMEC) at an appropriate level in the engagement space in order to create one or more specific effects in support of an objective. --TOPFAS Conceptual Data Model |

### 7. Actor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| # | 7 |
| Name | Actor |
| Qualified Name | INTEL-FS Spiral 2::NATO::JISR::Battlespace::Object::Actor::Actor |
| Type | `Type:<Class>` |
| Description | A person or a group of persons that is able to perform actions. [MIM-5.1]In the context of Operations Planning, Actors are defined as persons, institutions, organizations, governments, or states that are seen as a system in the context of the Knowledge Base Development function. What this definition does not tell is Actors are the controllers injecting inputs to the system. In other words, actors aim to control the state of the system-of-systems by carrying out actions with resources that are available to them. This implies that only resources owned or controlled by actors can perform actions. All deliberate actions in the Engagement Space must be performed by resources that belong to a defined actor. -- OPERATIONS PLANNING CORE DOMAIN MODEL Edition 1.1An Actors is person or organization, including state and non-state entities, with the capability to pursue its interests and objectives. [COPD] |
| Source URI | platform:/resource/INTEL-FS/INTEL-FS%20CIM%20RDM.emx#_Oa00wLIDEeqGEZ5htVJYdw |
